posts.name: Jean Menwa
posts.cooked: …has the winemaking improved over the years? Also, since when (what yr.)? Would you consider it a decent value play in Bordeaux?

posts.name: Ashish
posts.cooked: I’ve had the 1995 and 2000 and both were excellent wines with the slight edge to the 2000. Both were good value plays, and good wines in general. So I don’t think it’s a recent phenomenon…

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: By virtue of its elder sibling it’s always going to be somewhat in a shadow, but there were years, such as (the especially good) 1990, where it has gone head to head with Mouton. And Mouton’s new winemaker was previously in charge of Clerc Milon, it’s worth noting.

posts.name: Jim Freeman
posts.cooked: There have been some good ones! Heck, it’s stablemate d’armhailac is an impressive drink with a few years of age.

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked: I am a big fan of Clerc Milon. It is a beautiful, elegant, refined Pauillac. You can find out everything you wanted to know and more about Clerc here if you like Learn about Chateau Clerc Milon Pauillac, Complete Guide

link_counts.title: Learn about Chateau Clerc Milon Pauillac, Complete Guide

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
There have been some good ones! Heck, it’s stablemate d’armhailac is an impressive drink with a few years of age.

1999 d’Armailhac is so much better than it has any right to be!

posts.name: Jayson Cohen
posts.cooked:
I’ve had the 1995 and 2000 and both were excellent wines with the slight edge to the 2000. Both were good value plays, and good wines in general. So I don’t think it’s a recent phenomenon…

I don’t know Clerc Milon well but agree on 1995. I bought it on release and it has aged well. The 1983 is also excellent.

posts.name: Richard Flack
posts.cooked:
There have been some good ones! Heck, it’s stablemate d’armhailac is an impressive drink with a few years of age.

+1. Personally I’ve always preferred D’Armailhac. Excellent value.

posts.name: Ramon Cabrera
posts.cooked:
…has the winemaking improved over the years? Also, since when (what yr.)? Would you consider it a decent value play in Bordeaux?

I don’t know about the value part, but the 2000, which I bought at release, is one good Clerc Milon.

posts.name: Scott Brunson
posts.cooked:
By virtue of its elder sibling it’s always going to be somewhat in a shadow, but there were years, such as (the especially good) 1990, where it has gone head to head with Mouton. And Mouton’s new winemaker was previously in charge of Clerc Milon, it’s worth noting.

I LOVED that 90; haven’t tasted one in a while though.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
I am a big fan of Clerc Milon. It is a beautiful, elegant, refined Pauillac. You can find out everything you wanted to know and more about Clerc here if you like Learn about Chateau Clerc Milon Pauillac, Complete Guide

Yup, 100% agree. Big fan of d’Armailhac as well. I should buy more Clerc Milon, but have lots of d’Armailhac across a fair number of vintages. Somehow I ended up with a case of 2012 at clearance of $20 per. A crazy price.

posts.name: Nathan Smyth
posts.cooked:
Back in the day, James Suckling was yuge on the 1995 CM, with 95pts @ $25 in the 1998 Top 100.

“Like smelling a delicious raspberry sauce with hints of coffee and smoke, and the wine that follows is wonderfully structured, muscular and well toned. Full-bodied, with sleek and racy tannins and a long, characterful aftertaste. One of the top buys of the vintage. Best after 2001. 15,000 cases made. —JS”

All Lists of Top 100 Wines | Wine Spectator's Top 100

link_counts.title: All Lists of Top 100 Wines | Wine Spectator's Top 100

posts.name: Ashish
posts.cooked:
Back in the day, James Suckling was yuge on the 1995 CM, with 95pts @ $25 in the 1998 Top 100.

“Like smelling a delicious raspberry sauce with hints of coffee and smoke, and the wine that follows is wonderfully structured, muscular and well toned. Full-bodied, with sleek and racy tannins and a long, characterful aftertaste. One of the top buys of the vintage. Best after 2001. 15,000 cases made. —JS”

Wine Spectator's Top 100 Wines from 2023 Back to 1998 | Wine Spectator's Top 100

That note has no resemblance to the wine I tasted. I liked my bottle better.

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
There have been some good ones! Heck, it’s stablemate d’armhailac is an impressive drink with a few years of age.

+1. Personally I’ve always preferred D’Armailhac. Excellent value.

Not that I’ve blind tasted them side by side, but generally I’ve liked d’Armailhac over Clerc Milon too.

Maybe it’s just the labels.

I remember a generation ago these were considered oakier Pauillacs but it all seems to be the style now. With time it gets soaked up though.

posts.name: Ian Howe
posts.cooked:
Somehow I ended up with a case of 2012 at clearance of $20 per. A crazy price.

(Sigh) How quickly they forget. That was me old buddy. I hooked you up with that one.

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
I am a big fan of Clerc Milon. It is a beautiful, elegant, refined Pauillac. You can find out everything you wanted to know and more about Clerc here if you like Learn about Chateau Clerc Milon Pauillac, Complete Guide

Yup, 100% agree. Big fan of d’Armailhac as well. I should buy more Clerc Milon, but have lots of d’Armailhac across a fair number of vintages.

Both wines are very good and in your wheelhouse. Though by 2009/2010, Clerc Milon became better than d’Armailhac . Clerc added new gravity cellars a few years ago. Today they offer more finesse and purity, plus they added a second wine.

Knowing you read my site on a daily basis you were already aware of that.
